---
title: "Lab10"
author: "Vandana Brahmasa"
date: "2025-04-22"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

```{r}
###Load data function
load_data <- function(data_file){
load(url(paste0(r'{https://web.ics.purdue.edu/~zeng135/}', data_file, '.rdata')),
envir = .GlobalEnv)}
load_data("Shiller")
str(Shiller)
head(Shiller)
```
```{r}
###Plot the price column
plot(Shiller[,1])
```
c.  Calculate the monthly returns on the index. Note that Price is ex-dividend, so you would need to add
the dividends back in calculating the returns. Also Dividend is annualized, so you would need to divide it by
12 to get the monthly dividend. Plot the returns. What is your observation?
```{r}
monthly_dividend <- Shiller$Dividend/12
lagged_prices <- lag(Shiller$Price, k=1)
monthly_returns <- (Shiller$Price + monthly_dividend) / lagged_prices
plot(monthly_returns,
     main= "monthly returns",
     ylab= "Return",
     xlab= "Year",
     type= 'l',
     col= "blue")
```
High volatility during the Great Depression, negative peaks observed in 2008 and 2020 around financial crashes

d. Estimate the expected return by calculating the average return.

```{r}
expected_monthly_return <- mean(monthly_returns, na.rm=TRUE)
expected_monthly_return
```
e. Calculate the standard error of the expected return estimate. This is just the standard deviation divided
by the square root of the sample size.
```{r}
standard_deviation <- sd(monthly_returns, na.rm = TRUE)
n <- length(monthly_returns)
standard_error <- standard_deviation/sqrt(n)
standard_error
```
f. Calculate the 95% confidence interval of the expected return estimate. What do you think of the accuracy
of this estimate?
```{r}
###Confidence Interval at 95%= average returns+/- z score* Standard error
z_score <- 1.96 ###Corresponding to 95%
lower_limit <- expected_monthly_return- z_score *standard_error
upper_limit <-  expected_monthly_return+ z_score* standard_error
CI <- c("Lower 95%"= lower_limit, "Upper 95%"= upper_limit)
CI
```
g. Repeat steps d, e, and f, using the sub-sample after World War II. What are the trade-offs between using
a more recent sub-sample versus using the whole sample
```{r}
###Filter post WW2 sample:
Shiller_postWWII <- Shiller["1946/"]
Shiller_postWWII_monthly_dividend <- Shiller_postWWII$Dividend/12
monthly_returns_postWWII <- (Shiller_postWWII$Price+ Shiller_postWWII_monthly_dividend/ lag(Shiller_postWWII$Price))
monthly_returns_postWWII
```
```{r}
###expected return
expected_return_postWWII <- mean(monthly_returns_postWWII, na.rm= TRUE)
expected_return_postWWII
```
```{r}
###Standard Error
se_postWWII <- sd(monthly_returns_postWWII, na.rm = TRUE)/ sqrt(length(monthly_returns_postWWII))
se_postWWII
```
```{r}
###CI
z_score <- 1.96 ###Corresponding to 95%
lower_limit <- expected_return_postWWII- z_score *se_postWWII
upper_limit <-  expected_return_postWWII+ z_score* se_postWWII

CI <- c("Lower 95%"= lower_limit, "Upper 95%"= upper_limit)
CI
```
